1:
"Then Agrippa said unto Paul, Thou art permitted to speak for thyself. Then Paul stretched forth the hand, and answered for himself:" The witnesses note Agrippa granting Paul leave to speak. Matthew Henry notes Agrippa was the most honourable person in the assembly, having the title of king; Festus having opened the cause, Agrippa, as the mouth of the court, gave Paul licence to speak; and "Paul was silent till he had this liberty allowed him; for those are not the most forward to speak that are best prepared to speak and speak best." Gill notes this followed Festus's introduction of the affair. The witnesses note Paul, given leave by Agrippa, stretching forth his hand and beginning his defence — the apostle waiting for liberty to speak, then beginning with dignity. The witnesses note the leave to speak. The witnesses note Paul's dignified beginning.

The witnesses note Agrippa granting Paul leave, and Paul beginning his defence — the apostle waiting for liberty, then speaking with dignity.

So the witnesses make the verse Agrippa granting Paul leave to speak. The lesson the expositors draw is the dignity and order of Paul's defence, waiting for liberty before he spoke. The witnesses note the leave: "Agrippa said unto Paul, Thou art permitted to speak for thyself" — Agrippa, as the chief person and the mouth of the court (Matthew Henry notes), gave Paul licence to speak; the formal permission; the apostle granted his hearing. The expositors observe Paul's waiting: Matthew Henry notes Paul was silent till he had this liberty allowed him; "those are not the most forward to speak that are best prepared to speak and speak best"; the apostle, well-prepared, yet waiting for leave; the order and patience; the dignity that does not rush. The witnesses press the dignified beginning: "Paul stretched forth the hand, and answered for himself" — Paul stretched out his hand (the orator's gesture) and began his defence; the dignified, composed manner; the apostle, before a king, beginning with grace; the calm beginning. The expositors draw the lesson on the order of Paul's conduct: the apostle waited for permission, then spoke with dignity; the orderly, courteous manner; the believer's conduct before rulers marked by order and grace; the dignity of the apostle. The witnesses observe the occasion: Paul stood before Agrippa (a king), Festus (the governor), and the assembly; the great audience; the apostle given the opportunity to preach Christ to kings and rulers; "before governors and kings for my sake" (Matthew 10:18); the providence of the platform. The expositors note the composure of Paul: the apostle, a prisoner, yet composed and dignified before the king; the calm of the innocent; the confidence of one prepared by the Spirit; the composure that grace gives. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Agrippa's leave, Paul's waiting, and the dignified beginning of the defence. The verse thus records Agrippa granting Paul leave to speak — and Paul beginning his defence — teaching the dignity and order of the apostle, waiting for liberty before he spoke.
2:
"I think myself happy, king Agrippa, because I shall answer for myself this day before thee touching all the things whereof I am accused of the Jews:" The witnesses note Paul's gracious introduction. Gill notes this was a handsome and artful way of introducing his defence, gaining the king's attention, yet not mere flattery; for it had been Paul's unhappiness that his case was not understood — neither Lysias nor Felix nor Festus knew the rites and customs of the Jews; but Agrippa was master of them, which Paul looked on as in his favour. Barnes notes Paul esteemed it a favour to make his defence before one who could understand the case. The witnesses note Paul professing himself happy to defend before Agrippa — the gracious, honest introduction, glad to plead before one who understood the Jewish questions. The witnesses note the gracious introduction. The witnesses note the happiness to be understood.

The witnesses note Paul's gracious introduction — happy to defend before Agrippa, who understood the Jewish questions.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's gracious introduction. The lesson the expositors draw is the honest, gracious opening, glad to plead before one who could understand the case. The witnesses note the profession: "I think myself happy, king Agrippa, because I shall answer for myself this day before thee" — Paul professes himself happy to make his defence before Agrippa; the gracious opening; the courteous introduction. The expositors observe Gill's note on its honesty: this was a handsome, artful way of gaining the king's attention, yet not mere flattery; for it had been Paul's unhappiness that his case was not understood — neither Lysias, Felix, nor Festus knew the Jewish rites and customs; but Agrippa was master of them; the honest ground of the gladness. The witnesses press the happiness to be understood: Barnes notes Paul esteemed it a favour to defend before one who could understand the case; the apostle glad to plead before a knowledgeable judge; the case, turning on Jewish questions (the Messiah, the resurrection), needing an understanding hearer; the happiness of being understood. The expositors draw the lesson on the contrast with flattery: where Tertullus flattered Felix falsely (Acts 24:2-4), Paul's introduction was honest — grounded in the real advantage of Agrippa's knowledge; the truthful courtesy; the difference between flattery and honest grace; the apostle's integrity. The witnesses observe the wisdom of the opening: Paul, by noting Agrippa's knowledge, both honoured the king and prepared him to weigh the Jewish questions fairly; the wisdom that conciliates honestly; the gracious, prudent introduction; the apostle's tact. The expositors note the providence in the understanding judge: God gave Paul, at last, a hearer who understood the case; the providence of the knowledgeable judge; the apostle's case to be fairly weighed; the providence in the platform. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's gracious introduction, the happiness to be understood, and the honest courtesy. The verse thus records Paul's gracious introduction — happy to defend before Agrippa — teaching the honest, gracious opening, glad to plead before one who could understand the case.
3:
"Especially because I know thee to be expert in all customs and questions which are among the Jews: wherefore I beseech thee to hear me patiently." The witnesses note Paul's appeal to Agrippa's expertise. Barnes notes Paul could say this without falsehood or flattery; Agrippa was a Jew, had spent much time in the region, and was acquainted with the customs and questions (the inquiries between Pharisees, Sadducees, and scribes). Gill notes Agrippa was expert in all the rites and ceremonies, whether of the law of Moses or the elders. The witnesses note Paul appealing to Agrippa's expertise in Jewish customs and questions, and begging a patient hearing — the honest ground of his confidence, and the courteous request to be heard fully. The witnesses note the appeal to expertise. The witnesses note the request for patience.

The witnesses note Paul appealing to Agrippa's expertise and begging a patient hearing — the honest ground of his confidence.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's appeal to Agrippa's expertise. The lesson the expositors draw is the honest appeal to the king's knowledge and the courteous request for a patient hearing. The witnesses note the appeal: "I know thee to be expert in all customs and questions which are among the Jews" — Paul appeals to Agrippa's expertise in the Jewish customs and questions; Barnes notes Paul could say this without falsehood or flattery, for Agrippa was a Jew, acquainted with the customs and the inquiries (between Pharisees, Sadducees, scribes); the honest acknowledgment. The expositors observe the truth of the appeal: Agrippa was indeed expert in the Jewish rites and questions (Gill notes, whether of the law of Moses or the elders); the appeal grounded in fact, not flattery; the honest acknowledgment of the king's knowledge; the truthful compliment. The witnesses press the advantage to Paul's case: because Agrippa understood the Jewish questions (the Messiah, the resurrection, the prophecies), he could weigh Paul's defence fairly; the expertise an advantage to the truth; the knowledgeable judge able to see the merit of the case; the advantage of understanding. The expositors draw the lesson on the request for patience: "I beseech thee to hear me patiently" — Paul courteously begs a patient hearing; the request for full attention; the apostle, having much to say (the conversion, the commission, the gospel), asking to be heard out; the courteous petition. The witnesses observe the wisdom of the appeal and request: Paul honours the king's knowledge and asks his patience; the wisdom that prepares the hearer; the prudent, courteous introduction; the apostle's tact. The expositors note the confidence in the truth: Paul, confident his case would bear the scrutiny of an expert, appeals to Agrippa's knowledge and asks a full hearing; the confidence of the truth; the apostle unafraid of the knowledgeable judge; the truth inviting examination. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's appeal to expertise, the request for patience, and the honest confidence in the truth. The verse thus records Paul's appeal to Agrippa's expertise — and the request for a patient hearing — teaching the honest appeal to the king's knowledge and the courteous request to be heard fully.
4:
"My manner of life from my youth, which was at the first among mine own nation at Jerusalem, know all the Jews;" The witnesses note Paul's appeal to his well-known early life. Barnes notes Paul refers to his opinions, principles, and conduct from his youth — the early part, when opinions and habits are formed; Paul, born in Tarsus, was sent early to Jerusalem for education in the school of Gamaliel (Acts 22:3); his manner of life was known to all the Jews. Gill notes his conduct and deportment from the time he could perform religious exercises. The witnesses note Paul appealing to his well-known manner of life from youth among the Jews at Jerusalem — the open, observed life that all the Jews could attest; the appeal to his known character. The witnesses note the known early life. The witnesses note the appeal to his character.

The witnesses note Paul appealing to his well-known early life among the Jews — the open, observed life all could attest.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's appeal to his well-known early life. The lesson the expositors draw is the appeal to his known character, open and observed from youth. The witnesses note the appeal: "My manner of life from my youth... among mine own nation at Jerusalem, know all the Jews" — Paul appeals to his manner of life from youth, known to all the Jews; Barnes notes his opinions, principles, and conduct from the early part, when habits are formed; the open, known life. The expositors observe the place of his upbringing: Paul, born in Tarsus, was sent early to Jerusalem for education in the school of Gamaliel (Acts 22:3); his youth spent among the Jews at Jerusalem; the manner of life formed there; the known upbringing. The witnesses press the openness of his life: Paul's manner of life was known to all the Jews — open, observed, public; not hidden; the Jews could attest his early character; the life lived in the open; the known conduct. The expositors draw the lesson on the appeal to known character: Paul appeals to his well-known early life as evidence — the Jews knew he had been a strict Pharisee (v.5), no secret heretic; the appeal to his observed character; the life that bore witness; the known integrity. The witnesses observe the force of the appeal: Paul, charged as an apostate and heretic, appeals to his well-known orthodox upbringing; the Jews knew he had been the strictest of Pharisees; the appeal refuting the charge of being a lifelong enemy of the law; the known life against the slander. The expositors note the wisdom of grounding the defence in known facts: Paul rests his defence on facts the Jews themselves knew; the appeal to common knowledge; the wise grounding in the verifiable; the truth that all could attest. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the known early life, the appeal to his character, and the open life that bore witness. The verse thus records Paul's appeal to his well-known early life — known to all the Jews — teaching the appeal to his known character, open and observed from youth.
5:
"Which knew me from the beginning, if they would testify, that after the most straitest sect of our religion I lived a Pharisee." The witnesses note Paul's appeal to his having been a strict Pharisee. Barnes notes the Jews knew Paul from the very commencement of his career, perfectly apprised of his whole course; "after the most straitest" (the most rigid, most strict) — not only in the written law but the traditions of the elders; Paul elsewhere testifies (Philippians 3:4-6) he had all the advantages of birth and training and had distinguished himself by his observance. The witnesses note Paul appealing to the Jews' knowledge that he had lived as a Pharisee, of the strictest sect — establishing his former orthodoxy, that his change was no light apostasy but a conviction wrought by God. The witnesses note the strict Pharisee. The witnesses note the former orthodoxy.

The witnesses note Paul appealing to his life as a strict Pharisee — establishing his former orthodoxy, the foil to his conversion.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's appeal to his having been a strict Pharisee. The lesson the expositors draw is the establishing of his former orthodoxy, the foil to the conviction that changed him. The witnesses note the appeal to their knowledge: "which knew me from the beginning, if they would testify" — the Jews knew Paul from the commencement of his career; Barnes notes they were perfectly apprised of his whole course; the witnesses who could testify; the known history. The expositors observe the strictness: "after the most straitest sect of our religion I lived a Pharisee" — Paul had lived as a Pharisee, of the strictest sect; Barnes notes "the most straitest" (the most rigid, most strict), not only in the written law but the traditions of the elders; the strict orthodoxy; the rigid observance. The witnesses press the establishing of his former orthodoxy: Paul had been the strictest of Pharisees (Philippians 3:4-6); no heretic, no enemy of the law, but the most zealous observer; the former orthodoxy established; the strict Pharisee. The expositors draw the lesson on the foil to his conversion: Paul's former strict orthodoxy is the foil to his change — for one so zealous for the law and the traditions to become a Christian was no light apostasy, but a conviction wrought by God; the great change the more striking; the foil to the conversion; only God could so turn the strictest Pharisee. The witnesses observe the appeal to the witnesses: "if they would testify" — Paul appeals to the Jews to testify to his former strictness; the witnesses who could confirm it; the known orthodoxy attested; the appeal to their knowledge. The expositors note the reluctance of the witnesses implied: "if they would testify" — Paul implies the Jews would be reluctant to testify to his former orthodoxy, for it told against their charge that he was a lifelong enemy of the law; the unwilling witnesses; the truth they would rather not own. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the strict Pharisee, the former orthodoxy, and the foil to his conversion. The verse thus records Paul's appeal to his life as a strict Pharisee — teaching the establishing of his former orthodoxy, the foil to the conviction that changed him.
6:
"And now I stand and am judged for the hope of the promise made of God unto our fathers:" The witnesses note Paul declaring he is judged for the hope of the promise. Barnes notes Paul stands arraigned, judged on the point on which all his nation are agreed — the hope of the promise; it is not quite certain whether he refers to the promise of the Messiah or the hope of the resurrection (before the sanhedrim, Acts 23:6, he said he was called in question for the hope of the resurrection). Gill notes the promise of the Messiah and salvation by him, and the resurrection and eternal glory. The witnesses note Paul declaring that he is on trial not for any crime, but for the hope of the promise God made to the fathers — the hope of the Messiah and the resurrection; the charge reduced to the national hope. The witnesses note the hope of the promise. The witnesses note the national hope.

The witnesses note Paul judged for the hope of the promise — the Messiah and the resurrection; the charge reduced to the national hope.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul judged for the hope of the promise. The lesson the expositors draw is that Paul was on trial for the national hope — the Messiah and the resurrection — not for any crime. The witnesses note the declaration: "now I stand and am judged for the hope of the promise made of God unto our fathers" — Paul declares he is on trial for the hope of the promise God made to the fathers; Barnes notes he stands arraigned on the point on which all his nation are agreed; the trial for the hope. The expositors observe the substance of the hope: Barnes notes it is not quite certain whether Paul refers to the promise of the Messiah or the hope of the resurrection; Gill notes the promise of the Messiah and salvation by him, and the resurrection and eternal glory; the hope embracing both — the Messiah come, and the resurrection through him; the national hope. The witnesses press the reduction of the charge to the hope: Paul shows that he is judged not for any crime, but for the hope of the promise — the very hope his nation shared; the charge dissolved into a question of the national hope; the trial for believing what the fathers were promised; the hope, not a crime. The expositors draw the lesson on the irony of the charge: Paul is accused by the Jews for holding the hope that God made to their fathers — the hope they themselves professed; the accusers prosecuting him for their own hope; the irony of the charge; the Jews opposing the fulfilment of their own promise. The witnesses observe the continuity with the fathers: the hope was "made of God unto our fathers" — the promise of old, the hope of Israel; Paul's faith the fulfilment of the fathers' hope; the continuity affirmed; the gospel the hope of Israel realized; "the hope of Israel" (Acts 28:20). The expositors note the wisdom of framing the charge as the national hope: Paul frames the trial as turning on the national hope, which Agrippa (a Jew) shared; the wise framing that brought the king to the heart of the matter; the charge set on common ground. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the hope of the promise, the national hope, and the irony of being judged for it. The verse thus records Paul judged for the hope of the promise — made to the fathers — teaching that Paul was on trial for the national hope, the Messiah and the resurrection, not for any crime.
7:
"Unto which promise our twelve tribes, instantly serving God day and night, hope to come. For which hope's sake, king Agrippa, I am accused of the Jews." The witnesses note the hope of the twelve tribes and the irony of the accusation. Barnes notes "our twelve tribes" was the name by which the Jews were designated; the ancient nation hoped to come to that promise; it had been the hope and expectation of the nation. Gill notes the promise of the Messiah, and of the resurrection and eternal glory following. The witnesses note Paul declaring that the twelve tribes, earnestly serving God, hope to attain this promise — yet he is accused for that very hope; the irony that the Jews prosecute him for the hope their whole nation cherishes. The witnesses note the hope of the tribes. The witnesses note the irony of the accusation.

The witnesses note the twelve tribes' hope and the irony — Paul accused for the very hope his whole nation cherishes.

So the witnesses make the verse the hope of the twelve tribes and the irony of the accusation. The lesson the expositors draw is the irony that Paul is accused for the very hope his whole nation earnestly cherishes. The witnesses note the national hope: "our twelve tribes, instantly serving God day and night, hope to come" — Paul declares that the twelve tribes (Barnes notes, the name for the whole Jewish nation), earnestly serving God day and night, hope to attain this promise; the hope of the whole nation; the national expectation. The expositors observe the earnestness: "instantly serving God day and night" — the tribes served God earnestly, constantly, in hope of the promise; the devout, persevering service in expectation; the nation's earnest hope; the constant devotion. The witnesses press the irony of the accusation: "for which hope's sake, king Agrippa, I am accused of the Jews" — Paul is accused by the Jews for the very hope their whole nation cherishes; the irony; the accusers prosecuting him for the hope they themselves earnestly serve God to attain; the absurdity of the charge. The expositors draw the lesson on the irony: the Jews, who served God day and night in hope of the promise, accused Paul for believing the promise was fulfilled in Christ; the accusers opposing the fulfilment of their own hope; the irony of prosecuting the man who found what they sought; the contradiction of the charge. The witnesses observe the continuity Paul affirms: Paul's faith was the fulfilment of the national hope, not a departure from it; the gospel the realization of what the tribes hoped for; the continuity; Paul believing what his nation served God to attain; the hope realized in Christ. The expositors note the appeal to Agrippa: "king Agrippa" — Paul appeals directly to the king, who as a Jew shared the national hope; the appeal to Agrippa's own understanding of the hope; the direct address bringing the king to weigh the irony; the personal appeal. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the hope of the tribes, the earnest service, and the irony of the accusation. The verse thus records the twelve tribes' hope and the irony of the accusation — teaching that Paul is accused for the very hope his whole nation earnestly cherishes.
8:
"Why should it be thought a thing incredible with you, that God should raise the dead?" The witnesses note Paul's challenge concerning the resurrection. Gill notes the doctrine of the resurrection was thought incredible by the heathen (laughed at by the Stoics and Epicureans at Athens) and by the Sadducees; Paul addresses either Festus and the heathen present, or Agrippa and the Sadducees in court, expostulating why it should be looked on as incredible. Barnes notes the appeal to the power of God. The witnesses note Paul challenging his hearers — why is it thought incredible that God should raise the dead? — the appeal to the power of God, who can surely raise the dead; the reasonableness of the resurrection. The witnesses note the challenge on the resurrection. The witnesses note the power of God.

The witnesses note Paul challenging why the resurrection is thought incredible — the appeal to God's power to raise the dead.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's challenge concerning the resurrection. The lesson the expositors draw is the reasonableness of the resurrection, grounded in the power of God. The witnesses note the challenge: "why should it be thought a thing incredible with you, that God should raise the dead?" — Paul challenges his hearers to consider why the resurrection should be thought incredible; the bold question; the appeal to reason and to the power of God. The expositors observe the audience addressed: Gill notes the resurrection was thought incredible by the heathen (laughed at by the Stoics and Epicureans at Athens, Acts 17:32) and by the Sadducees; Paul addresses either Festus and the heathen present, or Agrippa and the Sadducees in court; the challenge to the unbelievers. The witnesses press the appeal to God's power: the resurrection is not incredible if God is considered — for God, who made all things, can surely raise the dead; the appeal to the power of God; "is any thing too hard for the LORD?" (Genesis 18:14); the reasonableness grounded in omnipotence. The expositors draw the lesson on the reasonableness of the resurrection: the resurrection, thought incredible by the natural mind, is reasonable when God's power is considered; the God who created can recreate, who gave life can restore it; the resurrection no harder than creation; the power of God making it credible. The witnesses observe the connection with Christ's resurrection: Paul's challenge prepares for his testimony of the risen Christ (v.23); if God can raise the dead, the resurrection of Jesus is credible; the general truth supporting the particular; the resurrection of Christ the proof. The expositors note the rebuke of unbelief: Paul rebukes the unbelief that counts the resurrection incredible; the limiting of God by the natural mind; the failure to consider God's power; the unbelief reproved; "ye do err, not knowing the scriptures, nor the power of God" (Matthew 22:29). The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the challenge on the resurrection, the appeal to God's power, and the reasonableness of the hope. The verse thus records Paul's challenge concerning the resurrection — why thought incredible? — teaching the reasonableness of the resurrection, grounded in the power of God.
9:
"I verily thought with myself, that I ought to do many things contrary to the name of Jesus of Nazareth." The witnesses note Paul's confession of his former conviction against Jesus. Gill notes this is a correction of himself — why he should wonder at their unbelief, when this was once his own case; it was the real sentiment of his mind, what in his conscience he believed to be right. Barnes notes Paul here begins the account of his conversion, stating he was not originally disposed to be a Christian, but was violently opposed; he thought he ought to oppose the name of Jesus. The witnesses note Paul confessing that he sincerely thought he ought to act against the name of Jesus — the conscientious error, the zeal that opposed Christ in good faith; the foil to the grace that converted him. The witnesses note the former conviction. The witnesses note the conscientious error.

The witnesses note Paul confessing he sincerely thought he ought to oppose Jesus — the conscientious error, the foil to grace.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's confession of his former conviction against Jesus. The lesson the expositors draw is the conscientious error of the persecutor, the zeal that opposed Christ in good faith, the foil to the grace that changed him. The witnesses note the confession: "I verily thought with myself, that I ought to do many things contrary to the name of Jesus of Nazareth" — Paul confesses that he sincerely thought he ought to act against the name of Jesus; Gill notes it was the real sentiment of his mind, what in his conscience he believed to be right; the conscientious conviction. The expositors observe Gill's note on the self-correction: this is a correction of himself — why should he wonder at their unbelief, when this was once his own case; the apostle owning that he had been as they were; the foil to his own change; the understanding of their state from his own. The witnesses press the conscientious error: Paul thought he "ought" to oppose Jesus — a sincere but mistaken conviction; the zeal that opposed Christ in good faith, believing it right; the conscientious error; the sincerity that was yet wrong. The expositors draw the lesson on sincere error: Paul was sincere, but sincerely wrong; the conscience may be misled, the zeal misdirected; sincerity no proof of truth; "I did it ignorantly in unbelief" (1 Timothy 1:13); the conscientious error needing the grace that enlightens. The witnesses observe the foil to grace: Paul's sincere opposition to Christ is the foil to the grace that converted him — for one so conscientiously set against Jesus to be turned was the work of God; the great change the more striking; only grace could turn the sincere opponent; the foil to the conversion. The expositors note the warning against trusting sincerity: Paul's example warns that sincerity alone does not save; a man may be sincerely, conscientiously wrong; the need of the truth, not mere sincerity; the warning against resting in good intentions. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's former conviction, the conscientious error, and the foil to the grace that converted him. The verse thus records Paul's confession of his former conviction against Jesus — thinking he ought to oppose him — teaching the conscientious error of the persecutor and the foil to the grace that changed him.
10:
"Which thing I also did in Jerusalem: and many of the saints did I shut up in prison, having received authority from the chief priests; and when they were put to death, I gave my voice against them." The witnesses note Paul's confession of his violent persecution. Gill notes that in Jerusalem he consented to Stephen's death, held the witnesses' clothes, haled men and women to prison, and made havoc of the church. Barnes notes "gave my voice against them" — he voted, or concurred, in their condemnation. The witnesses note Paul confessing that he imprisoned the saints by authority of the chief priests, and voted for their deaths — the depth of his persecution, owned openly; the violent enemy of the church, the foil to the grace that saved him. The witnesses note the imprisonment of saints. The witnesses note voting for their deaths.

The witnesses note Paul confessing he imprisoned the saints and voted for their deaths — the depth of his persecution owned openly.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's confession of his violent persecution. The lesson the expositors draw is the depth of Paul's persecution, owned openly, the foil to the grace that saved him. The witnesses note the imprisonment: "many of the saints did I shut up in prison, having received authority from the chief priests" — Paul imprisoned many of the saints by authority of the chief priests; Gill notes he haled men and women to prison and made havoc of the church (Acts 8:3); the violent persecution. The expositors observe the voting for deaths: "when they were put to death, I gave my voice against them" — Paul voted, or concurred, in their condemnation; Barnes notes "gave my voice against them" means he voted for their death; the consenting to their execution; the blood guilt. The witnesses press the depth of the persecution: Paul imprisoned the saints, voted for their deaths, made havoc of the church; the violent, bloody persecution; the enemy of the church; the depth of his enmity; "I persecuted the church of God, and wasted it" (Galatians 1:13). The expositors draw the lesson on the open confession: Paul owns his persecution openly before the king — the imprisonment, the voting for deaths; the honest confession of his great sin; the penitent owning the past; the candour of the apostle; "the chief of sinners" (1 Timothy 1:15). The witnesses observe the foil to grace: Paul's bloody persecution is the foil to the grace that saved him — for such a persecutor to be made an apostle was the triumph of grace; the depth of the sin magnifying the mercy; the foil to the conversion; the grace abounding over the sin. The expositors note the authority from the chief priests: Paul acted by authority of the chief priests; the persecution sanctioned by the religious leaders; the official enmity; the leaders behind the persecution. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the imprisonment of saints, the voting for deaths, and the foil to the grace that saved the persecutor. The verse thus records Paul's confession of his violent persecution — imprisoning the saints, voting for their deaths — teaching the depth of his persecution, owned openly, the foil to the grace that saved him.
11:
"And I punished them oft in every synagogue, and compelled them to blaspheme; and being exceedingly mad against them, I persecuted them even unto strange cities." The witnesses note the extremity of Paul's persecution. Barnes notes "compelled them to blaspheme" — to deny that Jesus was the Messiah and reproach him as an impostor; it is not necessarily implied that he succeeded, but that he violently sought it; "exceedingly mad" — the rage of his persecution. Gill notes he punished them by beating and scourging in the synagogues. The witnesses note Paul confessing that he punished the saints in the synagogues, sought to compel them to blaspheme Christ, and in his fury pursued them even to foreign cities — the extremity of his rage; the violent persecutor, the foil to the grace that mastered him. The witnesses note the extremity of the persecution. The witnesses note the rage against the saints.

The witnesses note the extremity of Paul's persecution — compelling blasphemy, pursuing to foreign cities; the rage that grace mastered.

So the witnesses make the verse the extremity of Paul's persecution. The lesson the expositors draw is the rage of the persecutor, the extremity owned, the foil to the grace that mastered him. The witnesses note the punishment: "I punished them oft in every synagogue" — Paul punished the saints often in the synagogues; Gill notes by beating and scourging (Acts 22:19); the repeated, violent punishment; the persecution in the very places of worship. The expositors observe the compelling to blaspheme: "compelled them to blaspheme" — Barnes notes Paul sought to compel them to deny Jesus was the Messiah and reproach him as an impostor; it is not necessarily implied he succeeded, but that he violently sought it; the attempt to force apostasy; the cruelty of compelling blasphemy. The witnesses press the rage: "being exceedingly mad against them, I persecuted them even unto strange cities" — Paul, in exceeding rage, pursued the saints even to foreign cities (as Damascus); Barnes notes the rage of his persecution; the fury that knew no bounds; the persecutor's madness. The expositors draw the lesson on the extremity of the persecution: Paul's persecution reached its extremity — punishing, compelling blasphemy, pursuing to foreign cities; the rage that would not rest; the violent, relentless enmity; the depth of his sin. The witnesses observe the candour of the confession: Paul owns the extremity of his persecution — the rage, the compelling of blasphemy, the pursuit to foreign cities; the honest confession of the worst; the penitent owning the full measure; the candour of the apostle. The expositors note the foil to grace: Paul's exceeding madness against the saints is the foil to the grace that mastered him — for one so enraged to be subdued and converted was the triumph of grace; the rage overcome by mercy; the foil to the conversion; the grace that tamed the fury. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the extremity of the persecution, the rage against the saints, and the grace that mastered it. The verse thus records the extremity of Paul's persecution — compelling blasphemy, pursuing to foreign cities — teaching the rage of the persecutor, owned candidly, the foil to the grace that mastered him.
12:
"Whereupon as I went to Damascus with authority and commission from the chief priests," The witnesses note Paul on his errand of persecution to Damascus. Gill notes that being intent on punishing the saints, compelling them to blaspheme, imprisoning and putting them to death, Paul went to Damascus, the chief city of Syria, where he knew there were many believers. Matthew Henry notes the principle that those who act by God's direction and warrant are not to be opposed, for that is fighting against God. The witnesses note Paul going to Damascus, armed with authority from the chief priests, to persecute the believers there — the persecutor on his errand, at the height of his enmity, about to be met by Christ. The witnesses note the errand to Damascus. The witnesses note the height of his enmity.

The witnesses note Paul on his errand to Damascus, armed to persecute — the persecutor at the height of his enmity, about to meet Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul on his errand to Damascus. The lesson the expositors draw is the persecutor at the height of his enmity, armed for persecution, about to be met by Christ. The witnesses note the errand: "as I went to Damascus with authority and commission from the chief priests" — Paul went to Damascus, armed with authority from the chief priests, to persecute the believers; Gill notes he went to Damascus (the chief city of Syria) where he knew there were many believers; the persecutor's mission. The expositors observe the authority: "with authority and commission from the chief priests" — Paul was armed with official authority to arrest the believers; the sanctioned persecution; the persecutor empowered; the official errand. The witnesses press the height of his enmity: Paul, at the height of his rage (v.11), armed with authority, went to Damascus to ravage the church there; the persecutor at his fiercest; the enmity at its peak; the violent mission. The expositors draw the lesson on the sovereign grace about to meet him: Paul, on his errand of persecution, at the height of his enmity, was about to be met by Christ and converted; the persecutor stopped on his way; the sovereign grace that intervenes at the worst moment; the grace that arrests the enemy on his errand. The witnesses observe the dramatic setting: the persecutor, armed and confident, on the road to Damascus, about to be struck down and converted; the dramatic reversal at hand; the contrast between his intent and what would befall; the stage set for grace. The expositors note Matthew Henry's principle: those who act by God's direction and warrant are not to be opposed, for that is fighting against God; Paul, opposing the church, was fighting against God; the persecutor about to learn he fought against the Lord; the warning against opposing God's work. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's errand to Damascus, the height of his enmity, and the grace about to meet him. The verse thus records Paul on his errand to Damascus — armed to persecute — teaching the persecutor at the height of his enmity, about to be met by the sovereign grace of Christ.
13:
"At midday, O king, I saw in the way a light from heaven, above the brightness of the sun, shining round about me and them which journeyed with me." The witnesses note the Damascus road light, brighter than the sun. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes "at midday" (more vivid than the phrase in Acts 22:6), and "above the brightness of the sun" (here alone, indicating the supernatural character of the light). Gill notes Paul calls on Agrippa to attend, the matter being wonderful and important. The witnesses note Paul recounting the light from heaven, at noon, brighter than the sun, shining round him and his companions — the supernatural appearance, attested by its surpassing the noonday sun; the glory of Christ that arrested the persecutor. The witnesses note the light above the sun. The witnesses note the supernatural appearance.

The witnesses note the light brighter than the noonday sun — the supernatural appearance that arrested the persecutor.

So the witnesses make the verse the Damascus road light. The lesson the expositors draw is the supernatural character of the light, attested by its surpassing the noonday sun. The witnesses note the appeal to the king: "at midday, O king, I saw in the way a light from heaven" — Paul calls on Agrippa to attend (Gill notes, the matter being wonderful and important); the direct address; the recounting of the great event. The expositors observe the detail of the light: "above the brightness of the sun, shining round about me and them which journeyed with me" — the light, at noon, was brighter than the sun; Robertson notes "above the brightness of the sun" is here alone (not in Acts 9 or 22), indicating the supernatural character of the light; the surpassing brightness; the supernatural glory. The witnesses press the supernatural character: a light at noon, brighter than the noonday sun — no natural phenomenon; the supernatural appearance; the glory of the risen Christ; the light that surpassed the sun; the divine intervention. The expositors draw the lesson on the reality of the appearance: the light, brighter than the sun at noon, proves the appearance was no delusion, no natural event; the supernatural glory arresting Paul and his companions; the reality of the divine appearance; the certainty of the event. The witnesses observe that the companions also saw it: "shining round about me and them which journeyed with me" — the light shone on all; the companions witnesses to the appearance; the event public, attested; not Paul's private vision only, but seen by all; the corroborated reality. The expositors note the glory of Christ in the light: the surpassing light was the glory of the risen, exalted Christ; the brightness of the Lord's glory; the appearance of the glorified Jesus; the glory that struck down the persecutor. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the light above the sun, the supernatural appearance, and the glory of Christ that arrested Paul. The verse thus records the Damascus road light, brighter than the noonday sun — teaching the supernatural character of the appearance, the glory of Christ that arrested the persecutor.
14:
"And when we were all fallen to the earth, I heard a voice speaking unto me, and saying in the Hebrew tongue, Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me? it is hard for thee to kick against the pricks." The witnesses note the voice and the proverb of the pricks. Ellicott notes the genuineness of the reading "it is hard for thee to kick against the pricks" is here beyond doubt. Robertson notes "in the Hebrew language" — the voice spoke in Hebrew (Aramaic). The witnesses note the voice of Christ, in Hebrew, asking "why persecutest thou me?" and adding the proverb of the goads — "it is hard for thee to kick against the pricks" — the figure of the futility and self-harm of resisting God; Paul, like an ox kicking the goad, only wounded himself by opposing Christ. The witnesses note the searching question. The witnesses note the proverb of the pricks.

The witnesses note the voice and the proverb of the pricks — the futility and self-harm of resisting God, like an ox kicking the goad.

So the witnesses make the verse the voice and the proverb of the pricks. The lesson the expositors draw is the searching question and the figure of the futility and self-harm of resisting God. The witnesses note the fall and the voice: "when we were all fallen to the earth, I heard a voice... in the Hebrew tongue, Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me?" — Paul and his companions fell to the earth, and Paul heard the voice of Christ in Hebrew (Robertson notes the Aramaic), asking why he persecuted; the searching question (as Acts 9:4; 22:7); the persecution of the church identified with Christ. The expositors observe the proverb added here: "it is hard for thee to kick against the pricks" — Ellicott notes the genuineness of this reading is here beyond doubt; the proverb of the goads added in this account; the figure of resistance. The witnesses press the figure of the pricks: the "pricks" (goads) were sharp sticks used to drive oxen; an ox that kicked against the goad only wounded itself; so Paul, kicking against the goad of Christ (resisting his conscience, the truth, the convictions), only harmed himself; the futility and self-harm of resisting God; the figure of vain resistance. The expositors draw the lesson on resisting God: it is hard, futile, and self-harming to resist God — like the ox kicking the goad; the resistance vain, the kicker wounded; the warning against fighting against God (Acts 5:39); the folly of opposing the Lord. The witnesses observe the convictions Paul resisted: the "pricks" suggest Paul had been resisting convictions — perhaps the testimony of the Christians, the dying of Stephen, the truth pressing on his conscience; the goads of conviction he kicked against; the resistance to the truth; the conscience pricked. The expositors note the grace in the warning: Christ warns Paul of the futility of his resistance — not to destroy, but to turn him; the warning the door to conversion; the grace that exposes the folly of resistance to save; the goad turned to mercy. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the searching question, the proverb of the pricks, and the futility of resisting God. The verse thus records the voice and the proverb of the pricks — it is hard to kick against the goads — teaching the searching question and the futility and self-harm of resisting God.
15:
"And I said, Who art thou, Lord? And he said, I am Jesus whom thou persecutest." The witnesses note Christ's self-revelation as the persecuted One. Gill notes the reading "the Lord said" in some copies. The witnesses note Paul's question, "Who art thou, Lord?", and Christ's answer, "I am Jesus whom thou persecutest" — the risen Christ revealing himself as the very Jesus whom Paul persecuted, again identifying himself with his suffering church; the persecutor confronted with the living Lord he opposed. The witnesses note the question. The witnesses note Christ's self-revelation.

The witnesses note Christ revealing himself — "I am Jesus whom thou persecutest" — the persecutor confronted with the living Lord.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's self-revelation as the persecuted One. The lesson the expositors draw is the risen Christ revealing himself as the very Jesus whom Paul persecuted, identified with his suffering church. The witnesses note the question: "I said, Who art thou, Lord?" — Paul, arrested and fallen, asks who is speaking; the question of the awakened conscience; the persecutor seeking to know the One who addressed him. The expositors observe Christ's answer: "I am Jesus whom thou persecutest" — the risen Christ reveals himself as Jesus, the very One Paul persecuted; the self-revelation of the living Lord; the persecutor confronted with the Jesus he opposed. The witnesses press the identification with the church: "whom thou persecutest" — again Christ identifies himself with his persecuted people; Paul, persecuting the Christians, persecuted Jesus; the union of Christ and his church; the Head feeling his members' sufferings; "why persecutest thou me?" (v.14). The expositors draw the lesson on the resurrection proved: the appearance proved Jesus was risen, alive, glorified — not the dead impostor Paul supposed, but the living Lord; the resurrection demonstrated to the persecutor; the foundation of Paul's faith laid in this appearance; he "seen of me also" (1 Corinthians 15:8). The witnesses observe the confrontation: Paul, who thought Jesus dead and his cause crushed, is confronted with the living Christ — "I am Jesus"; the persecutor face to face with the One he opposed; the confrontation that shattered his unbelief; the living Lord before him. The expositors note the grace in the revelation: Christ reveals himself not to destroy but to convert; the self-revelation the means of Paul's conversion; the risen Lord meeting the persecutor with grace; the revelation that converts. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's question, Christ's self-revelation, and the persecutor confronted with the living Lord. The verse thus records Christ's self-revelation — I am Jesus whom thou persecutest — teaching the risen Christ revealing himself as the very Jesus Paul persecuted, identified with his suffering church.
16:
"But rise, and stand upon thy feet: for I have appeared unto thee for this purpose, to make thee a minister and a witness both of these things which thou hast seen, and of those things in the which I will appear unto thee;" The witnesses note Christ's commission of Paul as minister and witness. Gill notes these words (not in the former accounts) bid Saul rise and take heart, for though he had acted so cruelly, Christ had designs of grace toward him; the appearance was not for his destruction but for his honour and usefulness. The witnesses note Christ raising Paul and appointing him a minister and witness of what he had seen and of what Christ would yet reveal — the commission of the persecutor to the apostleship; grace not only pardoning but employing the enemy. The witnesses note the commission. The witnesses note the persecutor made a minister.

The witnesses note Christ commissioning Paul as minister and witness — grace not only pardoning but employing the persecutor.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's commission of Paul. The lesson the expositors draw is the grace that not only pardons but employs the persecutor, making him a minister and witness. The witnesses note the raising: "rise, and stand upon thy feet" — Christ bids Paul rise; Gill notes the words (not in the former accounts) bid Saul rise and take heart, for though he had acted so cruelly, Christ had designs of grace; the encouragement; the appearance not for destruction but for usefulness. The expositors observe the commission: "I have appeared unto thee for this purpose, to make thee a minister and a witness" — Christ appeared to make Paul a minister (a servant) and a witness; the appointment to the apostleship; the persecutor made a minister; the commission. The witnesses press the substance of the witness: "both of these things which thou hast seen, and of those things in the which I will appear unto thee" — Paul was to witness both of what he had now seen (the risen Christ) and of what Christ would yet reveal to him; the testimony grounded in the appearance and in further revelations; the witness of the seen and the to-be-revealed. The expositors draw the lesson on grace employing the enemy: Christ not only pardoned the persecutor, but made him a minister and witness; grace not only forgiving but employing; the enemy turned into the apostle; "that in me first Jesus Christ might shew forth all longsuffering, for a pattern" (1 Timothy 1:16); the grace that uses the converted enemy. The witnesses observe the encouragement in the raising: "rise, and stand upon thy feet" — Christ raises the fallen persecutor, bids him take heart; the grace that lifts up; the encouragement to the one who had sinned so greatly; the comfort of the commission. The expositors note the honour of the appointment: the persecutor, who deserved destruction, was appointed to the high honour of the apostleship; the grace that exalts the unworthy; the enemy made a minister and witness; the honour of grace. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Christ's commission, the persecutor made a minister, and the grace that employs the enemy. The verse thus records Christ's commission of Paul as minister and witness — teaching the grace that not only pardons but employs the persecutor.
17:
"Delivering thee from the people, and from the Gentiles, unto whom now I send thee," The witnesses note Christ's promise of deliverance and the sending to the Gentiles. Gill notes "the people" means the people of the Jews (distinguished from the Gentiles); for the Lord knew that as soon as Saul was converted and preached, the Jews would become his implacable enemies; so he promises deliverance beforehand. Barnes notes this implied Paul would be persecuted by both Jews and Gentiles, and the Lord would rescue him. The witnesses note Christ promising to deliver Paul from both Jews and Gentiles, and sending him to the Gentiles — the promise of protection amid persecution, and the commission to the nations; the apostle to the Gentiles sent and shielded. The witnesses note the promise of deliverance. The witnesses note the sending to the Gentiles.

The witnesses note Christ promising deliverance and sending Paul to the Gentiles — protection amid persecution, the apostle to the nations.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's promise of deliverance and the sending to the Gentiles. The lesson the expositors draw is the promise of protection amid persecution and the commission to the nations. The witnesses note the deliverance: "delivering thee from the people, and from the Gentiles" — Christ promises to deliver Paul from both the Jewish people and the Gentiles; Gill notes "the people" means the Jews, who would become Paul's implacable enemies once he preached; Barnes notes this implied Paul would be persecuted by both, and the Lord would rescue him; the promise of protection. The expositors observe the foreknowledge in the promise: Christ foreknew that Paul would be persecuted by Jews and Gentiles, and promised deliverance beforehand; the foreknowledge and the provision; the Lord forearming his servant with the promise; the deliverance foretold. The witnesses press the sending to the Gentiles: "unto whom now I send thee" — Christ sends Paul to the Gentiles; the commission to the nations; the apostle to the Gentiles; the sending; "I will send thee far hence unto the Gentiles" (Acts 22:21). The expositors draw the lesson on the protection amid persecution: Paul was sent into danger (persecution by Jews and Gentiles), but with the promise of deliverance; the servant shielded amid the danger; the Lord preserving his apostle for the work; "I am with thee, and no man shall set on thee to hurt thee" (Acts 18:10); the protection in the mission. The witnesses observe the commission defining Paul's life: the sending to the Gentiles defined Paul's apostleship; the apostle to the nations; the mission that shaped his life and travels; the commission to the Gentiles. The expositors note the comfort of the promise: amid the persecution he would face, Paul had the promise of Christ's deliverance; the comfort of the divine protection; the assurance sustaining the apostle; the promise upholding him. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the promise of deliverance, the sending to the Gentiles, and the protection amid persecution. The verse thus records Christ's promise of deliverance and the sending to the Gentiles — teaching the promise of protection amid persecution and the commission to the nations.
18:
"To open their eyes, and to turn them from darkness to light, and from the power of Satan unto God, that they may receive forgiveness of sins, and inheritance among them which are sanctified by faith that is in me." The witnesses note the great commission and its summary of conversion. Gill expounds "to open their eyes" — the eyes of the understanding, enlightening them to see their lost state, the sinfulness of sin, and Christ the only Saviour. The witnesses note Christ's commission summarizing the work of conversion — to open eyes, turn men from darkness to light and from Satan to God, that they receive forgiveness and inheritance among the sanctified by faith in Christ — the fullest statement of the gospel's saving effects, all by faith. The witnesses note the opening of eyes. The witnesses note the elements of conversion.

The witnesses note the great commission — opening eyes, turning from darkness to light and Satan to God, forgiveness and inheritance by faith.

So the witnesses make the verse the great commission and its summary of conversion. The lesson the expositors draw is the fullest statement of the gospel's saving effects, all received by faith in Christ. The witnesses note the opening of eyes: "to open their eyes, and to turn them from darkness to light" — Gill expounds the opening of the eyes of the understanding, enlightening men to see their lost state, the sinfulness of sin, and Christ the only Saviour; the turning from the darkness of sin and ignorance to the light of the gospel; the enlightenment and conversion. The expositors observe the deliverance from Satan: "and from the power of Satan unto God" — the turning from the power (dominion) of Satan to God; the deliverance from the kingdom of darkness; the rescue from Satan's dominion to God's; "translated us into the kingdom of his dear Son" (Colossians 1:13); the great transfer. The witnesses press the saving effects: "that they may receive forgiveness of sins, and inheritance among them which are sanctified" — the converted receive forgiveness of sins (the pardon) and an inheritance among the sanctified (the eternal portion); the blessings of salvation; pardon and inheritance; the gospel's gifts. The expositors draw the lesson on the elements of conversion: the verse summarizes the work — the opening of eyes (enlightenment), the turning from darkness to light and Satan to God (conversion), the forgiveness of sins (justification), the inheritance among the sanctified (glorification); the full sweep of salvation; the gospel's whole work; the fullest statement in Acts. The witnesses observe the key phrase "by faith that is in me": all these blessings — forgiveness, inheritance, sanctification — are received "by faith that is in me" (faith in Christ); faith the means; the saving effects all by faith in Christ; "justified by faith" (Romans 5:1); the instrument of the blessings. The expositors note the centrality of Christ: the whole work centers on Christ — the light, the object of faith, the giver of forgiveness and inheritance; the conversion to God through Christ; the faith in him the means; Christ the center of the gospel. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the opening of eyes, the elements of conversion, and the blessings received by faith in Christ. The verse thus records the great commission and its summary of conversion — opening eyes, turning from darkness and Satan, forgiveness and inheritance by faith — teaching the fullest statement of the gospel's saving effects, all received by faith in Christ.
19:
"Whereupon, O king Agrippa, I was not disobedient unto the heavenly vision:" The witnesses note Paul's obedience to the heavenly vision. Barnes notes that since the proof of Christ being the Messiah, of his resurrection, and of the calling was so clear, Paul deemed it his duty to engage in the work without delay; "I was not disobedient" — he was not unbelieving, but yielded himself and obeyed at once. Gill notes this followed the illustrious appearance and commission. The witnesses note Paul declaring that he was not disobedient to the heavenly vision — the prompt, full obedience to the divine call; the apostle yielding himself to Christ's commission without delay; obedience the response to the vision. The witnesses note the obedience to the vision. The witnesses note the prompt response.

The witnesses note Paul's obedience to the heavenly vision — the prompt, full yielding to Christ's call.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's obedience to the heavenly vision. The lesson the expositors draw is the prompt, full obedience to the divine call, the apostle yielding himself without delay. The witnesses note the declaration: "O king Agrippa, I was not disobedient unto the heavenly vision" — Paul declares to the king that he obeyed the heavenly vision; Barnes notes "I was not disobedient" means he was not unbelieving, but yielded and obeyed at once; the prompt obedience. The expositors observe the ground of the obedience: Barnes notes that since the proof of Christ being the Messiah, of his resurrection, and of the calling was so clear, Paul deemed it his duty to obey without delay; the clear proof requiring obedience; the evidence compelling the response; the warranted obedience. The witnesses press the promptness: Paul "was not disobedient" — he did not hesitate, delay, or resist, but yielded himself at once to Christ's commission; the prompt, immediate obedience; the apostle's ready response; the will surrendered. The expositors draw the lesson on obedience to the divine call: the right response to the heavenly vision was obedience; Paul yielded himself fully and at once; the obedience that the call requires; "Lord, what wilt thou have me to do?" (Acts 9:6); the prompt obedience of the converted. The witnesses observe the contrast with his former opposition: Paul, who had opposed Christ in good faith (v.9), now obeyed him at once; the rebel turned servant; the opponent become the obedient; the change in the man; the obedience replacing the opposition. The expositors note the defence in the obedience: Paul's point before Agrippa is that he had only obeyed the heavenly vision — he could do no other; the obedience his defence; the apostle doing what the vision commanded; "I must obey God"; the obedience justifying his course. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's obedience, the prompt response, and the yielding to the heavenly vision. The verse thus records Paul's obedience to the heavenly vision — I was not disobedient — teaching the prompt, full obedience to the divine call, the apostle yielding himself without delay.
20:
"But shewed first unto them of Damascus, and at Jerusalem, and throughout all the coasts of Judaea, and then to the Gentiles, that they should repent and turn to God, and do works meet for repentance." The witnesses note Paul's summary of his ministry and its message. Barnes notes this verse is a summary of his labours in obedience to the command; his argument is that the Lord had commanded him, and he had done no more than obey. Clarke notes he preached at Damascus and Arabia about three years before going to Jerusalem (Galatians 1:17-18). The witnesses note Paul recounting that he preached repentance and turning to God, with works meet for repentance, to Damascus, Jerusalem, Judea, and the Gentiles — the message of repentance and its fruit, preached everywhere in obedience to the commission. The witnesses note the message of repentance. The witnesses note works meet for repentance.

The witnesses note Paul preaching repentance and works meet for it — everywhere, in obedience to the commission.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's summary of his ministry and message. The lesson the expositors draw is the message of repentance and its fruit, preached everywhere in obedience to the commission. The witnesses note the scope of the ministry: "shewed first unto them of Damascus, and at Jerusalem, and throughout all the coasts of Judaea, and then to the Gentiles" — Paul preached to Damascus, Jerusalem, Judea, and the Gentiles; Barnes notes this is a summary of his labours in obedience to the command; the wide ministry; the gospel preached everywhere. The expositors observe the message: "that they should repent and turn to God" — Paul preached repentance and turning to God; the call to repentance; the turning from sin to God; the gospel summons; the message of conversion. The witnesses press the fruit required: "and do works meet for repentance" — Paul required works meet for (worthy of) repentance; the fruit of true repentance in a changed life; not mere profession, but works showing the repentance real; "bring forth therefore fruits meet for repentance" (Matthew 3:8); the proof of genuine repentance. The expositors draw the lesson on repentance and its fruit: true repentance turns to God and brings forth good works; the change of heart shown in the change of life; faith and repentance issuing in works; not works as the ground of salvation, but as the fruit and evidence of true repentance; the fruit that proves the root. The witnesses observe the obedience in the ministry: Paul's whole ministry was obedience to the commission; he preached as the Lord commanded; the apostle's defence that he had only obeyed; the ministry the fulfilment of the vision; the obedience continued. The expositors note the order of the ministry: "first... Damascus... Jerusalem... Judaea... then to the Gentiles" — the gospel preached first to the Jews, then to the Gentiles; the order of the mission; the Jew first, then the Gentile (Romans 1:16); the wide obedience. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the message of repentance, the works meet for it, and the ministry in obedience to the commission. The verse thus records Paul's summary of his ministry — preaching repentance and its fruit everywhere — teaching the message of repentance and its fruit, preached in obedience to the commission.
21:
"For these causes the Jews caught me in the temple, and went about to kill me." The witnesses note the true cause of the Jews' assault. Gill notes Paul means the Asiatic Jews, who finding him in the temple laid hold on him and dragged him out, and went about to kill him, for no other reason but for preaching to the Gentiles and the doctrines mentioned. Clarke notes the causes reduce to his maintaining the resurrection and preaching to the Gentiles. The witnesses note Paul declaring that for these causes — preaching repentance and the gospel to the Gentiles — the Jews seized him in the temple and sought to kill him; the true ground of the assault, the gospel itself; the persecution for the truth. The witnesses note the true cause. The witnesses note the assault for the gospel.

The witnesses note the true cause of the assault — Paul's preaching the gospel to the Gentiles; the persecution for the truth.

So the witnesses make the verse the true cause of the Jews' assault. The lesson the expositors draw is that the true ground of the assault was the gospel itself, the persecution for the truth. The witnesses note the cause: "for these causes the Jews caught me in the temple, and went about to kill me" — Paul declares that for these causes (his preaching repentance and the gospel, especially to the Gentiles) the Jews seized him in the temple and sought to kill him; Gill notes it was the Asiatic Jews, who laid hold on him for no other reason but his preaching to the Gentiles and the doctrines mentioned; the true ground. The expositors observe Clarke's summary of the causes: the causes reduce to Paul's maintaining the resurrection and his preaching to the Gentiles; the doctrines that provoked the Jews; the resurrection and the Gentile mission; the offence of the gospel. The witnesses press the true ground of the assault: the Jews seized Paul not for any crime, but for preaching the gospel — repentance, the resurrection, the Gentile mission; the persecution for the truth; the assault on account of the gospel; the gospel the real offence. The expositors draw the lesson on the persecution for the gospel: Paul was assaulted not for wrongdoing, but for preaching the truth; the gospel the cause of the persecution; the apostle suffering for the message; "all that will live godly in Christ Jesus shall suffer persecution" (2 Timothy 3:12); the persecution for righteousness. The witnesses observe the connection to his defence: Paul shows that the assault arose from his obedience to the commission (v.19-20) — he preached as commanded, and for this the Jews sought to kill him; the persecution the consequence of his obedience; the suffering for doing right; the defence grounded in his innocence. The expositors note the offence of the Gentile mission: the preaching to the Gentiles was the chief offence (as Acts 22:21-22); the Jewish exclusivism enraged; the Gentile mission the spark; the offence of grace to the nations. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the true cause of the assault, the persecution for the gospel, and the offence of the truth. The verse thus records the true cause of the assault — preaching the gospel to the Gentiles — teaching that the true ground was the gospel itself, the persecution for the truth.
22:
"Having therefore obtained help of God, I continue unto this day, witnessing both to small and great, saying none other things than those which the prophets and Moses did say should come:" The witnesses note Paul's testimony to God's help and the gospel's agreement with the Scriptures. Gill notes God had delivered Paul many times from Jews and Gentiles, according to his promise, and saved him from all their designs; therefore he could say he continued to this day. Barnes notes Paul witnessed to small and great. The witnesses note Paul attributing his preservation to God's help, and declaring that he taught nothing but what Moses and the prophets foretold — the divine preservation and the gospel's agreement with the Scriptures; Christianity the fulfilment of the Old Testament. The witnesses note God's help. The witnesses note the agreement with the Scriptures.

The witnesses note Paul's preservation by God's help and his gospel's agreement with Moses and the prophets — the fulfilment of Scripture.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's testimony to God's help and the gospel's agreement with Scripture. The lesson the expositors draw is the divine preservation of the apostle and the gospel's continuity with the Scriptures. The witnesses note the divine help: "having therefore obtained help of God, I continue unto this day" — Paul attributes his preservation to God's help; Gill notes God had delivered him many times from Jews and Gentiles, according to his promise (v.17), saving him from all their designs; the divine preservation; the help of God. The expositors observe the perseverance in witness: "witnessing both to small and great" — Paul continued witnessing to all, small and great (the lowly and the mighty, even now before the king); the persevering testimony; the witness to all ranks; the faithful continuance. The witnesses press the agreement with the Scriptures: "saying none other things than those which the prophets and Moses did say should come" — Paul taught nothing but what Moses and the prophets foretold; the gospel's agreement with the Scriptures; Christianity the fulfilment of the Old Testament, not a departure; the continuity affirmed. The expositors draw the lesson on the gospel as the fulfilment of Scripture: Paul preached only what Moses and the prophets foretold — the suffering and resurrection of the Messiah (v.23), the light to the Gentiles; the gospel the fulfilment of the Scriptures; the continuity with the Old Testament; "to him give all the prophets witness" (Acts 10:43); the gospel no novelty but the fulfilment. The witnesses observe the defence in this agreement: Paul shows that his preaching was not contrary to the Jewish Scriptures, but their fulfilment; the gospel grounded in Moses and the prophets; the defence against the charge of heresy; the continuity vindicating the gospel. The expositors note the preservation for the work: God's help preserved Paul "unto this day" for the continued witness; the providence keeping the apostle for the work; the preservation serving the ministry; "the Lord stood with me" (2 Timothy 4:17); the help sustaining the witness. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks God's help, the agreement with the Scriptures, and the gospel as the fulfilment. The verse thus records Paul's testimony to God's help and the gospel's agreement with Scripture — teaching the divine preservation and the gospel's continuity with Moses and the prophets.
23:
"That Christ should suffer, and that he should be the first that should rise from the dead, and should shew light unto the people, and to the Gentiles." The witnesses note the substance of the prophetic gospel. Gill notes "that Christ should suffer" was recorded by Moses and the prophets — the meaning of the passover, the brazen serpent, the sacrifices, and the constant account of the prophets; the sufferer is Christ, in his human nature, though sufferings may be ascribed to his whole person. The witnesses note the threefold substance — that Christ should suffer, rise first from the dead, and bring light to Jews and Gentiles — the gospel foretold by Moses and the prophets, the suffering, risen, light-giving Messiah; the heart of Paul's testimony. The witnesses note the suffering Christ. The witnesses note the risen, light-giving Christ.

The witnesses note the prophetic gospel — the suffering, risen, light-giving Christ — foretold by Moses and the prophets.

So the witnesses make the verse the substance of the prophetic gospel. The lesson the expositors draw is the threefold substance of the gospel foretold by Moses and the prophets — the suffering, risen, light-giving Messiah. The witnesses note the suffering: "that Christ should suffer" — Gill notes this was recorded by Moses and the prophets (the passover, the brazen serpent, the sacrifices, the constant account of the prophets); the sufferer is Christ, in his human nature; the suffering Messiah foretold; "the LORD hath laid on him the iniquity of us all" (Isaiah 53:6). The expositors observe the resurrection: "that he should be the first that should rise from the dead" — Christ the first to rise from the dead (to die no more); the firstfruits of them that slept (1 Corinthians 15:20); the risen Messiah; the resurrection foretold and accomplished. The witnesses press the light to Jews and Gentiles: "should shew light unto the people, and to the Gentiles" — Christ should bring light to the people (the Jews) and to the Gentiles; the light to all; "a light to lighten the Gentiles" (Luke 2:32; Isaiah 49:6); the light-giving Messiah; the gospel to all nations. The expositors draw the lesson on the prophetic gospel: the gospel — the suffering, risen, light-giving Christ — was foretold by Moses and the prophets; the heart of Paul's testimony; the gospel grounded in the Scriptures; the prophetic foundation; the continuity with the Old Testament. The witnesses observe the answer to the offence: the very things that offended the Jews (the suffering Messiah, the resurrection, the light to the Gentiles) were foretold by their own Scriptures; the offence answered by the prophets; the Gentile mission foretold; the Scriptures vindicating the gospel. The expositors note the centrality of Christ: the whole gospel centers on Christ — his suffering, his resurrection, his light to all; the Messiah the substance of the prophetic testimony; Christ the heart of the gospel; the center of the Scriptures. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the suffering Christ, the risen and light-giving Christ, and the gospel foretold by Moses and the prophets. The verse thus records the substance of the prophetic gospel — the suffering, risen, light-giving Christ — teaching the threefold gospel foretold by Moses and the prophets, the heart of Paul's testimony.
24:
"And as he thus spake for himself, Festus said with a loud voice, Paul, thou art beside thyself; much learning doth make thee mad." The witnesses note Festus's interruption charging Paul with madness. Matthew Henry notes Paul likely had much more to say in defence of the gospel, but Festus interrupted him. Barnes notes the happy turn Paul gives to this accusation; Festus, a heathen, hearing the doctrines of the resurrection and the light to the Gentiles, thought Paul's much learning had made him mad. The witnesses note Festus, unable to follow the spiritual truths, crying out that Paul's great learning had made him mad — the natural man counting the gospel folly; the spiritual truths beyond the carnal mind. The witnesses note the charge of madness. The witnesses note the natural man's verdict.

The witnesses note Festus charging Paul with madness — the natural man counting the gospel folly, the spiritual truths beyond him.

So the witnesses make the verse Festus's charge of madness. The lesson the expositors draw is the natural man counting the gospel folly, the spiritual truths beyond the carnal mind. The witnesses note the interruption: "as he thus spake for himself, Festus said with a loud voice, Paul, thou art beside thyself" — Festus interrupted Paul, charging him with being beside himself; Matthew Henry notes Paul likely had much more to say, but Festus interrupted; the abrupt charge; the interruption. The expositors observe the charge of madness: "much learning doth make thee mad" — Festus thought Paul's great learning had made him mad; Barnes notes Festus, a heathen, hearing the doctrines of the resurrection and the light to the Gentiles, thought Paul deranged; the charge of madness; the heathen's verdict. The witnesses press the natural man's incapacity: Festus, the carnal man, could not follow the spiritual truths — the resurrection, the suffering and risen Messiah, the light to the Gentiles; the truths beyond his comprehension; the natural man counting the gospel folly; "the natural man receiveth not the things of the Spirit of God: for they are foolishness unto him" (1 Corinthians 2:14); the spiritual truths beyond the carnal mind. The expositors draw the lesson on the gospel as folly to the natural man: the spiritual truths of the gospel appear madness to the unconverted; the wisdom of God counted folly by the world; the preaching of the cross foolishness to them that perish (1 Corinthians 1:18); the natural man's blindness; the gospel beyond the carnal. The witnesses observe the irony of the charge: Festus charged the most reasonable of men with madness; the truth counted madness; the sane apostle called mad by the blind judge; the irony; the world calling the wise mad. The expositors note the contrast with Agrippa: Festus (the heathen) thought Paul mad; Agrippa (the Jew, who knew the Scriptures) was almost persuaded (v.28); the difference between the ignorant and the knowing; the heathen's verdict against the Jew's conviction; the gospel received differently. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Festus's charge of madness, the natural man's verdict, and the gospel counted folly. The verse thus records Festus's charge of madness — much learning doth make thee mad — teaching the natural man counting the gospel folly, the spiritual truths beyond the carnal mind.
25:
"But he said, I am not mad, most noble Festus; but speak forth the words of truth and soberness." The witnesses note Paul's calm denial of madness. Gill notes Paul gave Festus his title of honour, not out of fear or flattery but according to custom; though Festus used him reproachfully, Paul denied being mad, and what he had said showed the reverse. Barnes notes the happy turn — Paul might have dwelt on his argument, but instead calmly affirms his words are truth and soberness. The witnesses note Paul, with calm dignity, denying madness and affirming that he spoke words of truth and soberness — the composure of the apostle, answering the charge with grace and asserting the rational, sober truth of the gospel. The witnesses note the calm denial. The witnesses note the words of truth and soberness.

The witnesses note Paul calmly denying madness — his words truth and soberness; the composure of the apostle.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's calm denial of madness. The lesson the expositors draw is the composure of the apostle, answering the charge with grace and asserting the rational, sober truth of the gospel. The witnesses note the courteous denial: "I am not mad, most noble Festus" — Paul calmly denies madness, giving Festus his title of honour; Gill notes Paul gave the title not out of fear or flattery but according to custom; though Festus used him reproachfully, Paul answered with courtesy; the calm, courteous denial.
