The expositors observe the assertion of truth: "but speak forth the words of truth and soberness" — Paul affirms that he speaks words of truth (true) and soberness (rational, sound); the gospel not madness but sober truth; the rational, well-grounded message; the truth and soundness asserted. The witnesses press the composure of the apostle: Paul, charged with madness, does not lose his composure, but calmly and courteously denies it; the dignity of the apostle; the calm of the innocent; the grace under accusation; the composure of grace. The expositors draw the lesson on the rationality of the gospel: the gospel is "words of truth and soberness" — not madness, not enthusiasm, but reasonable, sound truth; the gospel rational, well-grounded; "soberness" (sound mind) against the charge of madness; the reasonable faith. The witnesses observe the grace in the answer: Paul, reproached, answers with courtesy ("most noble Festus"); the meekness that returns grace for reproach; the gentle answer; the spirit of Christ in the apostle; "in meekness instructing those that oppose" (2 Timothy 2:25). The expositors note the contrast of the verdicts: Festus charged madness; Paul asserts truth and soberness; the world's verdict against the apostle's; the gospel called mad by the natural man, affirmed sober by the apostle; the contrast of judgments. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's calm denial, the words of truth and soberness, and the composure of the apostle. The verse thus records Paul's calm denial of madness — I speak truth and soberness — teaching the composure of the apostle and the rational, sober truth of the gospel.
26:
"For the king knoweth of these things, before whom also I speak freely: for I am persuaded that none of these things are hidden from him; for this thing was not done in a corner." The witnesses note Paul's appeal to Agrippa's knowledge of the facts. Gill notes the king knew something of the sufferings and resurrection of the Messiah, and of his showing light to Jews and Gentiles, as spoken by Moses and the prophets, and as reported fulfilled in Jesus; at least he had heard the report. Barnes notes Agrippa, many years in the region, knew of Jesus and of Paul's conversion. The witnesses note Paul appealing to Agrippa's knowledge, speaking freely, since these things were public — "not done in a corner"; the facts of the gospel open and notorious, known to the king. The witnesses note the appeal to the king's knowledge. The witnesses note the public facts.

The witnesses note Paul appealing to Agrippa's knowledge — the gospel facts public, "not done in a corner."

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's appeal to Agrippa's knowledge. The lesson the expositors draw is the public, notorious nature of the gospel facts, known to the king. The witnesses note the appeal: "the king knoweth of these things, before whom also I speak freely" — Paul appeals to Agrippa's knowledge of the facts; Gill notes the king knew of the sufferings and resurrection of the Messiah, and of the report of their fulfilment in Jesus; Barnes notes Agrippa, many years in the region, knew of Jesus and of Paul's conversion; the appeal to the king's knowledge. The expositors observe the freedom of speech: "before whom also I speak freely" — Paul speaks freely, boldly, confident the king knows the facts; the bold speech; the apostle unafraid before one who knew the truth; the freedom of the confident. The witnesses press the public nature of the facts: "I am persuaded that none of these things are hidden from him; for this thing was not done in a corner" — the gospel facts (the life, death, resurrection of Jesus, the spread of the gospel) were public, notorious, not done in secret; the open facts; "not done in a corner"; the public events. The expositors draw the lesson on the public, attested facts of the gospel: the events of the gospel were public, open, notorious — not secret or obscure; the facts attested, known; the gospel grounded in public events; the open, verifiable history; "not done in a corner." The witnesses observe the appeal to the open record: Paul appeals to the public knowledge of the facts — the king and all knew them; the gospel resting on attested, open events; the appeal to what all could know; the public record. The expositors note the contrast with Festus's ignorance: Festus (the heathen) thought Paul mad; but Agrippa (the Jew, in the region) knew the facts; the appeal from the ignorant to the knowing; the king able to attest what the governor could not; the knowing judge. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the appeal to the king's knowledge, the public facts, and the gospel not done in a corner. The verse thus records Paul's appeal to Agrippa's knowledge — not done in a corner — teaching the public, notorious nature of the gospel facts, known to the king.
27:
"King Agrippa, believest thou the prophets? I know that thou believest." The witnesses note Paul's direct appeal to Agrippa's faith in the prophets. Barnes notes this bland personal address is an instance of Paul's happy manner of appeal; he does it to bring in Agrippa's testimony to meet the charge of Festus that he was deranged; "believest thou the prophets?" — the prophecies of the Messiah's character, sufferings, and death; "I know that thou believest" — Agrippa, being a Jew, believed the prophets. The witnesses note Paul turning to Agrippa and pressing home the question of his faith in the prophets, then answering it for him — the bold, personal appeal that drove the argument to the king's own conscience; the prophets believed, the gospel their fulfilment. The witnesses note the appeal to the prophets. The witnesses note the personal application.

The witnesses note Paul pressing Agrippa on his faith in the prophets — the bold personal appeal to the king's conscience.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's direct appeal to Agrippa's faith in the prophets. The lesson the expositors draw is the bold, personal appeal that drove the argument home to the king's own conscience. The witnesses note the question: "King Agrippa, believest thou the prophets?" — Paul turns directly to the king and asks whether he believes the prophets; Barnes notes the bland personal address, an instance of Paul's happy manner of appeal; the direct question; the appeal to the king's faith. The expositors observe the purpose of the appeal: Barnes notes Paul does this to bring in Agrippa's testimony to meet the charge of Festus that he was deranged; the king's faith in the prophets would refute the charge of madness; the appeal turning the argument; the personal application. The witnesses press the answer Paul gives: "I know that thou believest" — Paul answers his own question, knowing Agrippa, a Jew, believed the prophets; the bold assertion of the king's faith; the appeal pressed home; the king committed by his own belief. The expositors draw the lesson on the force of the appeal: if Agrippa believed the prophets, and the prophets foretold the suffering, risen, light-giving Messiah (v.22-23), then Agrippa must consider whether Jesus fulfilled them; the argument driven to the king's conscience; the prophets believed, the gospel their fulfilment; the appeal that cornered the king. The witnesses observe the boldness of the personal application: Paul, a prisoner, boldly presses the king on his faith and its implications; the courage of the apostle; the personal, searching application; the gospel brought home to the individual; the bold appeal. The expositors note the wisdom of the appeal: Paul appeals to what Agrippa already believed (the prophets) to lead him to what he ought to believe (the gospel); the wisdom that builds on common ground; the argument from the accepted to the contested; the skillful appeal. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's appeal to the prophets, the personal application, and the argument driven to the king's conscience. The verse thus records Paul's direct appeal to Agrippa's faith — believest thou the prophets? — teaching the bold, personal appeal that drove the gospel home to the king's own conscience.
28:
"Then Agrippa said unto Paul, Almost thou persuadest me to be a Christian." The witnesses note Agrippa's famous reply. Gill takes it spoken seriously rather than ironically, arising from convictions of mind he could not stifle nor conceal. Barnes notes Agrippa could not deny he believed the prophets, nor that they had been fulfilled in Jesus, nor the evidence of Paul's conversion; instead of charging derangement, he candidly avows the impression the proof had made; "almost" (en oligō) — within a little, except a very little. The witnesses note Agrippa, convicted, confessing that Paul almost persuaded him to be a Christian — the famous near-persuasion, the conviction that stopped short of conversion; the tragedy of the "almost." The witnesses note the near-persuasion. The witnesses note the conviction that stopped short.

The witnesses note Agrippa's famous "almost thou persuadest me" — the near-persuasion, the conviction that stopped short of conversion.

So the witnesses make the verse Agrippa's famous reply. The lesson the expositors draw is the tragedy of the near-persuasion, the conviction that stopped short of conversion. The witnesses note the reply: "Almost thou persuadest me to be a Christian" — Agrippa confesses that Paul almost persuaded him; Gill takes it spoken seriously, arising from convictions he could not stifle; Barnes notes Agrippa could not deny the prophets, their fulfilment, or the evidence of Paul's conversion, and candidly avows the impression; "almost" (en oligō, within a little); the near-persuasion. The expositors observe the conviction: Agrippa was convicted — he could not deny the truth of Paul's argument; the proof had made an impression; the king moved, almost persuaded; the conviction real; the truth reaching him. The witnesses press the tragedy of the "almost": Agrippa was "almost" persuaded — moved, convicted, near the kingdom — yet stopped short of conversion; the near-persuasion that did not become faith; the tragedy of the "almost"; the conviction that did not issue in decision; "thou art not far from the kingdom of God" (Mark 12:34), yet not in it. The expositors draw the lesson on the danger of being "almost" a Christian: Agrippa's "almost" is a solemn warning — to be almost persuaded, yet not persuaded; convicted, yet not converted; near the kingdom, yet outside; the tragedy of stopping short; the "almost" that perishes; the peril of conviction without decision. The witnesses observe the hindrances that held Agrippa back: convicted, yet Agrippa did not yield — perhaps held by his sins (his incestuous life with Bernice), his pride, his position, the presence of the company; the hindrances to the decision; the cost he would not pay; the world holding him back. The expositors note the solemnity of the moment: Agrippa stood at the door of the kingdom, almost persuaded; yet (so far as we know) he turned away; the solemn moment of decision passed by; the conviction grieved away; the "almost" that became "never"; the warning to all who are almost persuaded. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Agrippa's near-persuasion, the conviction that stopped short, and the tragedy of the "almost." The verse thus records Agrippa's famous reply — almost thou persuadest me — teaching the tragedy of the near-persuasion, the conviction that stopped short of conversion.
29:
"And Paul said, I would to God, that not only thou, but also all that hear me this day, were both almost, and altogether such as I am, except these bonds." The witnesses note Paul's gracious wish for the salvation of all his hearers. Barnes notes this shows Paul's intense desire that Agrippa and all who heard might be saved, and his steady belief that none but God could incline them to become Christians; he sought it of God earnestly. Gill notes the prayer shows his affection for the souls of men and his desire for their conversion. The witnesses note Paul wishing that all his hearers might become, not almost, but altogether such as he was — Christians — yet without his bonds; the apostle's love for souls, the gracious wish, the exception of his chains the only gentle touch. The witnesses note the wish for all. The witnesses note the love for souls.

The witnesses note Paul's gracious wish — that all might be altogether Christians, except his bonds; the apostle's love for souls.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's gracious wish for the salvation of all. The lesson the expositors draw is the apostle's love for souls, wishing all to be altogether Christians. The witnesses note the wish: "I would to God, that not only thou, but also all that hear me this day, were both almost, and altogether such as I am" — Paul wishes that all his hearers might become, not almost, but altogether such as he was (a Christian); Barnes notes the intense desire that all might be saved; Gill notes the prayer shows his affection for souls; the gracious wish. The expositors observe the answer to Agrippa's "almost": Agrippa said "almost" (v.28); Paul answers with the wish that he and all might be "altogether" Christians; the apostle pressing beyond the "almost" to the "altogether"; the desire for full conversion, not the near-persuasion; the wish for the complete. The witnesses press the love for souls: "all that hear me this day" — Paul wishes the salvation not of Agrippa only, but of all present; the heart that longs for the salvation of all; the love that embraces every hearer; the apostle's compassion; "my heart's desire and prayer to God for Israel is, that they might be saved" (Romans 10:1). The expositors draw the lesson on the desire for full conversion: Paul wishes all to be "altogether" Christians — not almost, but wholly; the desire for the complete, saving faith, not the partial conviction; the apostle pressing for the full surrender; the wish for the "altogether." The witnesses observe the gracious exception: "except these bonds" — Paul wishes all to be as he is, except his chains; the gentle, gracious touch; the apostle wishing them his faith and joy, but not his sufferings; the love that would spare them his bonds; the grace of the wish. The expositors note the dependence on God in the wish: Barnes notes Paul's steady belief that none but God could incline them to become Christians; the wish addressed to God ("I would to God"); the conversion God's work; the apostle praying, knowing only God could save; the sovereign grace acknowledged. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's wish for all, the love for souls, and the gracious exception of his bonds. The verse thus records Paul's gracious wish — that all might be altogether Christians, except his bonds — teaching the apostle's love for souls and the desire for the full conversion of all his hearers.
30:
"And when he had thus spoken, the king rose up, and the governor, and Bernice, and they that sat with them:" The witnesses note the rising of the assembly. Chrysostom observes that again they pass sentence in his favour — having said "thou art beside thyself," they now acquit him as undeserving not only of death but of bonds, and would have released him entirely had he not appealed to Caesar; "but this was done providentially, that he should also depart with bonds," for if his Lord was reckoned among the transgressors (Mark 15:28), much more he. The witnesses note the king, governor, Bernice, and assembly rising — the hearing ended; the great ones withdrawing to confer; the providence that the innocent apostle, though acquitted in their hearts, would yet go to Rome in bonds. The witnesses note the rising. The witnesses note the providence in the bonds.

The witnesses note the assembly rising — the hearing ended; the providence that the acquitted apostle would yet go to Rome in bonds.

So the witnesses make the verse the rising of the assembly. The lesson the expositors draw is the end of the hearing and the providence that the innocent apostle would yet go to Rome in bonds. The witnesses note the rising: "the king rose up, and the governor, and Bernice, and they that sat with them" — the king, governor, Bernice, and assembly rose; the hearing ended; the great ones withdrawing to confer; the close of the proceeding. The expositors observe Chrysostom's note on the favourable sentence: again they pass sentence in Paul's favour — having said "thou art beside thyself" (v.24), they now acquit him as undeserving not only of death but of bonds; the verdict of innocence; the apostle cleared in their judgment. The witnesses press the providence in the bonds that Chrysostom marks: they would have released Paul entirely had he not appealed to Caesar; but this was done providentially, that he should also depart with bonds; the providence ordering that the innocent apostle, though acquitted in their hearts, would yet go to Rome in chains; the bonds providential. The expositors draw the lesson on the fellowship with Christ in the bonds: Chrysostom notes that if Paul's Lord was reckoned among the transgressors (Mark 15:28; Isaiah 53:12), much more would the servant be; the apostle, like his Master, numbered with the transgressors; the bonds a fellowship with Christ's sufferings; "the fellowship of his sufferings" (Philippians 3:10); the servant as the Master. The witnesses observe the providence in the appeal to Caesar: Paul's appeal to Caesar (Acts 25:11) would carry him to Rome in bonds; the providence using the appeal to bring the gospel to Rome; the bonds the means of the journey; "so must thou bear witness also at Rome" (Acts 23:11); the providence in the chains. The expositors note the marvel Chrysostom marks: the marvellous thing was Paul's being mixed up with such (the great, the corrupt) and yet receiving no harm from them; as the Lord did not share their character, so neither did Paul; the apostle among the great, untainted; the integrity preserved. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the rising of the assembly, the favourable verdict, and the providence in the bonds. The verse thus records the rising of the assembly — the hearing ended — teaching the providence that the innocent apostle, though acquitted in their hearts, would yet go to Rome in bonds, in fellowship with his Lord.
31:
"And when they were gone aside, they talked between themselves, saying, This man doeth nothing worthy of death or of bonds." The witnesses note the verdict of innocence. Barnes notes this was the conclusion after hearing all the Jews alleged; we have the concurring testimony of Claudius Lysias (Acts 23:29), Felix (Acts 24), Festus (Acts 25:26-27), and now Agrippa, to Paul's innocence — more honourable and satisfactory testimony Paul could not have desired; a full acquittal from all the charges. The witnesses note the king, governor, and assembly, in private, agreeing that Paul had done nothing worthy of death or bonds — the unanimous verdict of innocence from the highest authorities; the apostle fully vindicated. The witnesses note the verdict of innocence. The witnesses note the full vindication.

The witnesses note the unanimous verdict — Paul had done nothing worthy of death or bonds; the apostle fully vindicated.

So the witnesses make the verse the verdict of innocence. The lesson the expositors draw is the full vindication of Paul, the unanimous verdict of innocence from the highest authorities. The witnesses note the verdict: "they talked between themselves, saying, This man doeth nothing worthy of death or of bonds" — the king, governor, and assembly, in private, agreed that Paul had done nothing deserving death or imprisonment; the verdict of innocence; the apostle cleared. The expositors observe the weight of the testimony that Barnes marks: this was the conclusion after hearing all the Jews alleged; we have the concurring testimony of Claudius Lysias (Acts 23:29), Felix (Acts 24), Festus (Acts 25:26-27), and now Agrippa, to Paul's innocence; the repeated, concurring verdicts; the full attestation. The witnesses press the completeness of the vindication: Barnes notes more honourable and satisfactory testimony Paul could not have desired; a full acquittal from all the charges; the apostle fully vindicated by the highest authorities; the innocence established; the complete clearing. The expositors draw the lesson on the vindication of the innocent: God vindicated his servant — the highest authorities, after full hearing, declared him innocent; the providence clearing the apostle; the truth prevailing; "he shall bring forth thy righteousness as the light" (Psalm 37:6); the innocent vindicated. The witnesses observe the testimony to the gospel: the verdict cleared not only Paul but the gospel he preached — no crime found in the apostle of Christ; the gospel vindicated in its preacher; the innocence of the apostle a testimony to the truth; the gospel cleared. The expositors note the contrast with the continued bonds: though declared innocent, Paul would still go to Rome in bonds (having appealed to Caesar); the innocent yet bound; the verdict not freeing him, but attesting his innocence; the providence ordering the journey despite the acquittal. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the verdict of innocence, the full vindication, and the testimony to the gospel. The verse thus records the verdict of innocence — nothing worthy of death or bonds — teaching the full vindication of Paul, the unanimous verdict of innocence from the highest authorities.
32:
"Then said Agrippa unto Festus, This man might have been set at liberty, if he had not appealed unto Caesar." The witnesses note Agrippa's declaration and the providence of the appeal. Barnes notes this is a full declaration of Agrippa's conviction that Paul was innocent, and an instance where boldness and fidelity are attended with happy results; Paul had concealed nothing, had made a bold appeal (v.27) to Agrippa, who was not offended but the more impressed with his innocence. Gill notes Agrippa spoke his sense by way of advice, the matter resting with Festus. The witnesses note Agrippa declaring that Paul might have been freed had he not appealed to Caesar — the final testimony to his innocence, yet the providence of the appeal carrying him to Rome; the chains the path to the wider witness. The witnesses note the declaration. The witnesses note the providence of the appeal.

The witnesses note Agrippa's declaration that Paul might have been freed but for his appeal — the providence carrying him to Rome.

So the witnesses make the verse Agrippa's declaration and the providence of the appeal. The lesson the expositors draw is the final testimony to Paul's innocence and the providence of the appeal carrying him to Rome. The witnesses note the declaration: "This man might have been set at liberty, if he had not appealed unto Caesar" — Agrippa declares that Paul might have been freed had he not appealed to Caesar; Barnes notes this is a full declaration of Agrippa's conviction that Paul was innocent; the final testimony to his innocence; the king's verdict. The expositors observe the happy result of Paul's boldness that Barnes marks: Paul had concealed nothing, had made a bold appeal (v.27) to Agrippa, who was not offended but the more impressed with his innocence; the boldness and fidelity attended with happy results; the faithful witness vindicated; the bold appeal blessed. The witnesses press the providence of the appeal: Paul had appealed to Caesar (Acts 25:11); though this now seemed to prolong his bonds, it was the providence carrying him to Rome; the appeal the means of the wider witness; the chains the path to Rome; "so must thou bear witness also at Rome" (Acts 23:11). The expositors draw the lesson on the providence overruling the appeal: what seemed to delay Paul's freedom was the providence ordering his journey to Rome; the appeal, made for his protection, became the means of carrying the gospel to the capital; the providence in the appeal; the bonds the path to the wider mission; God's purpose in the chains. The witnesses observe the irony Agrippa notes: Paul might have been freed, but for his own appeal; the apostle, by appealing to Caesar, had (humanly) prolonged his bonds; yet this was the providence; the seeming loss the divine gain; the appeal that bound him the means of his greater witness. The expositors note the close of the hearing and the chapter: the hearing ended with the verdict of innocence and the declaration that the appeal must carry Paul to Rome; the stage set for the voyage (Acts 27); the apostle, innocent, bound for Rome; the providence directing the next scene; the journey ordained. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Agrippa's declaration, the testimony to innocence, and the providence of the appeal carrying Paul to Rome. The verse thus records Agrippa's declaration — Paul might have been freed but for his appeal — teaching the final testimony to his innocence and the providence of the appeal carrying him to Rome for the wider witness.
